entries.openingLines: The night Max wore his wolf suit and made mischief of one kind and another his mother called him "WILD THING!" and Max said "I'LL EAT YOU UP!" so he was sent to bed without eating anything.
entries.title: Where the Wild Things Are
entries.author: Maurice Sendak

entries.openingLines: In the light of the moon a little egg lay on a leaf.
entries.title: The Very Hungry Caterpillar
entries.author: Eric Carle

entries.openingLines: "Where's Papa going with that ax?" said Fern to her mother as they were setting the table for breakfast.
entries.title: Charlotte's Web
entries.author: E.B. White

entries.openingLines: Here is Edward Bear, coming downstairs now, bump, bump, bump, on the back of his head, behind Christopher Robin.
entries.title: Winnie-the-Pooh
entries.author: A.A. Milne

entries.openingLines: The sun did not shine. It was too wet to play. So we sat in the house all that cold, cold, wet day.
entries.title: The Cat in the Hat
entries.author: Dr. Seuss

entries.openingLines: In the great green room there was a telephone and a red balloon and a picture of the cow jumping over the moon.
entries.title: Goodnight Moon
entries.author: Margaret Wise Brown

entries.openingLines: Once there were four children whose names were Peter, Susan, Edmund and Lucy.
entries.title: The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe
entries.author: C.S. Lewis

entries.openingLines: Alice was beginning to get very tired of sitting by her sister on the riverbank, and of having nothing to do: once or twice she had peeped into the book her sister was reading, but it had no pictures or conversations in it, 'and what is the use of a book', thought Alice, 'without pictures or conversation?'
entries.title: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland
entries.author: Lewis Carroll

entries.openingLines: All children, except one, grow up.
entries.title: Peter Pan
entries.author: J.M. Barrie

entries.openingLines: Dorothy lived in the midst of the great Kansas prairies, with Uncle Henry, who was a farmer, and Aunt Em, who was the farmer's wife.
entries.title: The Wonderful Wizard of Oz
entries.author: L. Frank Baum

entries.openingLines: Mr and Mrs Brown first met Paddington on a railway platform. In fact, that was how he came to have such an unusual name for a bear, for Paddington was the name of the station.
entries.title: Paddington Bear
entries.author: Michael Bond

entries.openingLines: "Once upon a time there were four little Rabbits, and their names were— Flopsy, Mopsy, Cotton-tail, and Peter."
entries.title: The Tale of Peter Rabbit
entries.author: Beatrix Potter

entries.openingLines: It was a dark and stormy night.
entries.title: A Wrinkle in Time
entries.author: Madeleine L'Engle

entries.openingLines: This is a story about something that happened long ago when your grandfather was a child. It is a very important story because it shows how all the comings and goings between our own world and the land of Narnia first began.
entries.title: The Chronicles of Narnia: The Magician's Nephew
entries.author: C.S. Lewis

entries.openingLines: Ba-room, ba-room, ba-room, baripity, baripity, baripity, baripity—Good. His dad had the pickup going.
entries.title: Bridge to Terabithia
entries.author: Katherine Paterson

entries.openingLines: This story begins within the walls of a castle, with the birth of a mouse. A small mouse, the last of his litter and the only one who survived. And the last thing that the mother mouse said, before she died, was this: "He is such a disappointment."
entries.title: The Tale of Despereaux
entries.author: Kate DiCamillo

entries.openingLines: My name is India Opal Buloni, and last summer my daddy, the preacher, sent me to the store for a box of macaroni-and-cheese, some white rice, and two tomatoes and I came back with a dog.
entries.title: Because of Winn-Dixie
entries.author: Kate DiCamillo

entries.openingLines: I am Ivan. I am a gorilla. It's not as easy as it looks.
entries.title: The One and Only Ivan
entries.author: Katherine Applegate

entries.openingLines: "Harriet was trying to explain to Sport how to play Town. 'See, first you make up the name of the town. Then you write down the names of all the people who live in it.'"
entries.title: Harriet the Spy
entries.author: Louise Fitzhugh

entries.openingLines: My dear Saxonberg: I have been thinking about you and about Jamie and Claudia, and I have decided to give you the whole story — not just a part of it.
entries.title: From the Mixed-Up Files of Mrs. Basil E. Frankweiler
entries.author: E.L. Konigsburg

entries.openingLines: "Mrs. Rachel Lynde lived just where the Avonlea main road dipped down into a little hollow, fringed with alders and ladies' eardrops and traversed by a brook that had its source away back in the woods of the old Cuthbert place; it was reputed to be an intricate, headlong brook in its earlier course through those woods, with dark secrets of pool and cascade; but by the time it reached Lynde's Hollow it was a quiet, well-conducted little stream, for not even a brook could run past Mrs. Rachel Lynde's door without due regard for decency and decorum; it probably was conscious that Mrs. Rachel Lynde was sitting at her window, keeping a sharp eye on everything that passed, from brooks and children up, and that if she noticed anything odd or out of place she would never rest until she had ferreted out the whys and wherefores thereof."
entries.title: Anne of Green Gables
entries.author: L.M. Montgomery

entries.openingLines: When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen.
entries.title: The Secret Garden
entries.author: Frances Hodgson Burnett

entries.openingLines: "Christmas won't be Christmas without any presents," grumbled Jo, lying on the rug.
entries.title: Little Women
entries.author: Louisa May Alcott

entries.openingLines:
The tramp steamer Drake plowed away from the coast of India and pushed 
 its blunt prow into the Arabian Sea, homeward bound. Slowly it made its way
 west toward the Gulf of Aden. Its hold was loaded with coffee, rice, tea, oil 
seeds and jute. Black smoke poured from its one stack, darkening the hot 
cloudless sky
entries.title: The Black Stallion
entries.author: Walter Farley

entries.openingLines: Way out at the end of a tiny little town was an old overgrown garden, and in the garden was an old house, and in the house lived Pippi Longstocking.
entries.title: Pippi Longstocking
entries.author: Astrid Lindgren

entries.openingLines: Ramona Quimby hoped her parents would forget to give her a little talking-to.
entries.title: Ramona Quimby, Age 8
entries.author: Beverly Cleary

entries.openingLines: "When Mrs. Frederick C. Little's second son arrived, everybody noticed that he was not much bigger than a mouse. The truth of the matter was, the baby looked very much like a mouse in every way. He was only about two inches high; and he had a mouse's sharp nose, a mouse's tail, a mouse's whiskers, and the pleasant, shy manner of a mouse."
entries.title: Stuart Little
entries.author: E.B. White

entries.openingLines: It's a funny thing about mothers and fathers. Even when their own child is the most disgusting little blister you could ever imagine, they still think that he or she is wonderful.
entries.title: Matilda
entries.author: Roald Dahl

entries.openingLines: These two very old people are the father and mother of Mr. Bucket. Their names are Grandpa Joe and Grandma Josephine.
entries.title: Charlie and the Chocolate Factory
entries.author: Roald Dahl

entries.openingLines: Until he was four years old, James Henry Trotter had a happy life. He lived peacefully with his mother and his father in a beautiful house beside the sea.
entries.title: James and the Giant Peach
entries.author: Roald Dahl

entries.openingLines: Sophie couldn't sleep. A brilliant moonbeam was slanting through a gap in the curtains. It was shining right across her bed and onto the white wall opposite.
entries.title: The BFG
entries.author: Roald Dahl

entries.openingLines: When I left my office that beautiful spring day, I had no idea what was in store for me.
entries.title: Where the Red Fern Grows
entries.author: Wilson Rawls

entries.openingLines: Thirteen-year-old Brian Robeson maintained his white-knuckle grip on the small plane's controls.
entries.title: Hatchet
entries.author: Gary Paulsen

entries.openingLines: It was almost December, and Jonas was beginning to be frightened.
entries.title: The Giver
entries.author: Lois Lowry

entries.openingLines: Look, I didn't want to be a half-blood. If you're reading this because you think you might be one, my advice is: close this book right now. Believe whatever lie your mom or dad told you about your birth, and try to lead a normal life.
entries.title: Percy Jackson and the Olympians: The Lightning Thief
entries.author: Rick Riordan

entries.openingLines: Wind howled through the night, carrying a scent that would change the world.
entries.title: Eragon
entries.author: Christopher Paolini
